title: Iriyāvahiyaṁ Sutta — Confession of Faults Committed While Moving
summary: This is a short prayer of apology for any harm a person may have caused to living beings while walking or moving about — stepping on insects, seeds, sprouts, dew, mud, water, moss, or spider-webs. The reciter first resolves to atone, then names exactly what was harmed and in how many ways, and finally asks that the wrong be undone with the words micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ ('may my misdeed come to nothing'). It expresses the central Jain ideal of ahiṁsā (non-harm) and the careful awareness (īryā-samiti) one is meant to keep while in motion. It is recited again and again, because no movement is ever perfectly harmless.
ritualPlacement: Iriyāvahiyaṁ is the īryāpathika ('path-of-movement') confession that purifies the body's movement before one undertakes any major act of worship or pratikramaṇa. In this book's Daivasika (Devasi) flow it comes very early — after the opening Maṅgalācaraṇa/Navkar and Panchindiya (guru-establishment), it is recited and immediately followed by Tassa Uttari + Annattha and a one-Logassa kāyotsarga (see §3.1). The same sūtra is reused unchanged across all five pratikramaṇas (Devasi, Rāi, Pakkhī, Cāumāsī, Saṁvatsarī): it is defined once and referenced by each sequence. Note for the corpus generally: the pakkhī→cāumāsī→saṁvatsarī word-substitution governs other parts of the ritual, but Iriyāvahiyaṁ's own wording does not change between them; only its surrounding vidhi does.
authorAndSource: An Ardhamāgadhī Prakrit formula belonging to the canonical Āvaśyaka (Six Obligatory Duties) tradition; like the other core āvaśyaka sūtras it is received as an āgamic/gaṇadhara-transmitted text with no single named human author. In the present book it is printed verbatim in the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) edition of Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit. Precise individual authorship is not attested and is left as '—'.
structureArc: The seven verses move from intention, to act, to victim, and finally to release: (1) ichchāmi paḍikkamiuṁ — the opening resolve, 'I wish to atone'; (2) iriyāvahiyāe virāhaṇāe — names the fault as a transgression along the īryā-patha, the path of one's movement; (3) gamaṇāgamaṇe — narrows it to 'going and coming'; (4) the tenfold litany of what may be trodden — living creatures (pāṇa), seeds (bīya), greenery (hariya), dew (osā), small insects/ant-hills (uttiṅga), moss/mildew (paṇaga), water (daga), mud (maṭṭī) and spider-webs (makkaḍā-saṁtāṇā) — 'in trampling/crossing' (saṁkamaṇe); (5) je me jīvā virāhiyā — the pivot from acts done to beings harmed, 'whatever living beings I have injured'; (6) the fivefold classification of those beings by sense-faculty, from one-sensed to five-sensed (egindiya…pañcindiya); and (7) the tenfold list of *how* they were harmed — struck, covered with dust, crushed, collided, grazed, tormented, exhausted, terrified, displaced from place to place, and finally deprived of life — sealed by the formula tassa micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ.
recurringImagery: This sūtra works not by metaphor but by *analytic litany*: three expanding enumerations clamped by a single release-formula. First it widens the lens through three nested lists — the ten things one may tread on (v4, the objects of harm), the five sense-classes of beings (v6, the subjects of harm graded by how much life-faculty they possess), and the ten verbs of injury (v7, the modes of harm, ascending from a mere knock to taking life itself). Then it snaps the whole expanse shut with tassa micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ. Two devices recur and should be read the same way each time they appear: (a) the jīva-hierarchy by indriya-count, which assumes the Jain view that even earth, water, and plants hold one-sensed life, so that no footstep is doctrinally neutral; and (b) the micchā-mi-dukkaḍaṁ formula itself — not a plea to an external judge but a self-directed nullification, the wish that the harmful deed bear no fruit and bind no karma. The ascending order of v7 (from abhihaya, struck, to jīviyāo vavaroviya, killed) is deliberate: it confesses the full ladder of violence from the slightest to the gravest in one breath.
keyThemes: ahiṁsā — non-harm toward all living beings
īryā-samiti — vigilance over one's own movement
pratikramaṇa — turning back and atoning for transgression
the micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ formula of self-nullifying confession
the Jain classification of jīvas by number of senses
harm as unavoidable in motion, hence repeatedly confessed
purification of the body before higher worship

glossary.definition: Pertaining to the īryā-patha, the 'path of movement'; the confession for faults incurred while walking or moving.
Carefulness of movement — walking watchfully so as not to crush living beings; the first of the five samitis.
'Turning back' — the act of reflecting on and atoning for a transgression; here the reciter's resolve to repent (paḍikkamiuṁ).
Injury, violation, or harm done to living beings, whether deliberate or incidental.
Going and coming; movement to and fro, the occasion of the faults confessed.
A living being or soul; in Jain thought possessed even by earth, water, fire, air, and plant bodies.
Beings classified by sense-faculties: one-sensed (e.g. plants, earth-bodies) up to five-sensed (e.g. humans, animals).
Spider-webs (literally 'spiders' threads'), one of the things one may inadvertently disturb while moving.
Small ground-insects or ant-hills (variant glosses exist); included among things trodden underfoot.
Moss, mildew, or fungus — a one-sensed plant-body life-form easily crushed.
Struck or knocked — the first and lightest of the ten modes of injury listed in verse 7.
Deprived of life, killed — the gravest mode of injury, the culmination of verse 7.
'May my misdeed come to nothing' — the formula of self-nullifying confession that seals the sūtra.
Standing meditative abandonment of the body; the one-Logassa kāyotsarga that follows this sūtra in the flow.

howToRead: A newcomer can read this simply as a heartfelt apology — first the resolve to make amends, then a careful listing of what may have been hurt and how, ending with a request that the harm be undone. An advanced practitioner will hear the threefold analytic (act → being → mode), the doctrinal weight of the indriya-graded jīva hierarchy, and the ascending ladder of violence in verse 7, recited as the īryāpathika-pratikramaṇa that clears the body before any higher rite.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: verbatim sūtra text, ritual placement in the Devasi/Rāi/Pakkhī sequences, and the shared-sūtra model

sources.title: Āvaśyaka Sūtra tradition (Ṣaḍ-āvaśyaka) — canonical Prakrit obligatory-duty formulae
sources.consultedFor: classification of Iriyāvahiyaṁ as the īryāpathika confession and its place among the six āvaśyakas

notes: Several object-terms in verse 4 carry variant traditional glosses: osā is commonly 'dew' but is sometimes read as fine earth/moisture; uttiṅga is glossed as either small ground-insects/ant-hills or as a kind of moth; paṇaga as moss/mildew/fungus; daga (= udaka) as water and maṭṭī as mud/clay. The sūtra's wording is identical across all five pratikramaṇas; only the surrounding vidhi changes. Individual human authorship is not attested and is intentionally left unspecified. The source URLs above are placeholders for the named works; substitute stable verified URLs before publication rather than treating these as confirmed links.
